Lead.title: Lead
Lead.description: Represents a prospective client (property manager) who has submitted an AI Audit request through the landing page.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Lead entity.

name.description: The full name of the property manager requesting the AI audit.

email.description: The email address of the property manager.

rawInquiry.description: The original, raw text of the inquiry submitted by the property manager.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the lead inquiry was submitted.

isQualified.description: Boolean flag indicating whether the lead was qualified by the AI based on criteria like budget or move-in date.

aiResponse.description: The professional response generated by the AI for the lead, typically suggesting a viewing or next steps.

definition.description: Collection for storing all prospective client (property manager) leads who have submitted an AI Audit request. Access is restricted to authenticated 'Dublin AI Systems' staff. No specific 'ownerId' or 'members' map is needed within the Lead document itself as authorization is managed globally by the /agencyStaff collection.

Dedicated collection for identifying authorized 'Dublin AI Systems' staff members. The document ID corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID of a staff member. Used for global role-based access control to secure internal data like leads. Includes denormalized 'active' field (or similar) for explicit staff status.

params.name: userId
params.description: The Firebase Authentication UID of an agency staff member.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure is designed to adhere to the core principles of Authorization Independence, Clarity of Intent, DBAC, QAPs, and Invariants, specifically for the 'Lead' entity and agency staff access. All 'Lead' documents will reside in a top-level collection named `/leads/{leadId}`. Authorization for accessing these leads will be managed through a separate, dedicated collection for agency staff roles: `/agencyStaff/{userId}`. This collection will store documents where the document ID (`userId`) corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID of an authorized 'Dublin AI Systems' staff member. The document content can be minimal, e.g., `{ 'active': true }`.

**Authorization Independence:** This structure explicitly achieves Authorization Independence by completely decoupling lead document access from any hierarchical dependencies. Each `Lead` document in `/leads/{leadId}` does not need to store an `ownerId` or a `members` map for authorization purposes. Instead, access is determined by checking the `request.auth.uid` against the `/agencyStaff` collection. This allows `Lead` documents to be created, updated, and deleted atomically without requiring `get()` calls in security rules to check parent or related document attributes. The authorization context (who is an agency staff member) is denormalized into its own collection, making rules simple and efficient.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** This design effectively supports Query Access Patterns (QAPs) for secure `list` operations. By placing all `Lead` documents in the `/leads` collection and restricting read/list access solely to authenticated users whose UIDs exist in the `/agencyStaff` collection, Firestore security rules can enforce authorization at the collection level. When an agency staff member performs a query against `/leads`, the rules will first verify their staff status. If authorized, the query will proceed; if not, it will be rejected entirely. This prevents unauthorized clients from filtering out unprivileged data on the client side, ensuring that only data they are explicitly allowed to see is ever returned, fully adhering to the 'rules are not filters' principle. The homogeneous security posture of the `/leads` collection (all documents require staff access) further simplifies this.
